speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: this is tape number 4, December 1st, 1994, interview with Abraham Jachzel.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jachzel, I'd like to know, from you, how you were picked to go and work for the Schindler factory, where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I was in Plazow. And they always tried to keep the mechanics, the ones that really did know how to-- what to do-- how to do it, you know-- they kept to the end. And-- and since me and my father was there, and we were in the type of business, you know, which probably Schindler could have used. And we heard about Schindler, but it was in and out. At one time, he had a place, and they took away his people, brought them back to us. You know, it wasn't-- It wasn't clear. And then, you know, I wasn't-- I didn't know know too much about it Anyway, came in. We went. They took us. We-- we going to Schindler, some place in Czechoslovakia. And we wind up in Gross-Rosen, a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross-Rosen is in Germany-- I don't know, Silesia or whatever it is. I mean, you go by train, at night. What-- how much can you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: We came in there. And this is-- I thought, this is hell, you know? We got undressed, naked. And they look us that it-- every place you can look at, you know? Maybe we have something, a valuable. And we stood naked. And then it gets dark, and we're hungry, you know? Who were the soldiers who were looking at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German, German SS. And then, you know, when it got already in the evening, and then we-- they made-- they made bundles of 10. So you know what it is? It gets cold. And it's windy. And you stay naked.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody wants to be in the middle, to warm up, and-- and, you know, to change around. And every time you do make a movement, the guy with the whip hits somebody, you know? It was-- it was-- What month was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was like September, October, you know? In the daytime, the sun was shining. It was cool. But it wasn't gray. But we came at night. Anyway, [SIGHS] we finished. We were like 10 in a bundle. And we're standing there. And there is a chimney. But it's a one chimney. That's not a crematorium-- maybe yes, maybe no. We-- we didn't know exactly what it is. And then, you know, they take us in, 10 at a time. And we go in over there. And there is a guy, and he's shaving me in places I never knew-- known that those places you shave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a Frenchman. I start talking to him in German. So he answers in Jewish. He said, [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, what-- what's going to be with us? He said, don't worry. Once you here, you all right, you know? If not--

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was shower. And we really showered. And we came out half-- almost wet, nothing to-- to dry out. And then there was-- they took-- he said, they tell us to run to a barrack. And we ran into that barrack. You know, it was at night. And-- and I'm picking up-- it was a-- a-- how you-- those wooden legs, you know, from people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Prosthesis.

speaker: interviewee
text: --prosthesis and and stuff like this. I said, gosh, you know, this must be, you know, the people went in, you know, without a leg, without a hand. And they probably went straight to the crematorium, you know? And this is the stuff what's left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the barrack you were sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, when it came a little daytime, and I could see it, it was awful. I saw all that stuff, you know? I saw little dolls. You know, there-- there wasn't such-- there wasn't dolls like you see now, in United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, rag dolls and-- and-- and-- and the combs and-- and the shoes. You know, it was unbelievable. I said, that's it. How the hell are we going to make it here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then they took us. It was a beautiful barrack. You know, it's unbelievable. But, [LAUGHS], they didn't take us upstairs. They took us downstairs. Under the barrack, there was a big room. You know, I never saw a barrack it-- that had a second floor. And we went in there. And there-- and it-- and there comes breakfast. Oh, matter of fact, there was a swimming pool. That was a-- like a place to show, in case Americans or Germans or Swiss come in, to show them what a beautiful camp they got.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somebody told me there, they saw a head swimming in the swimming pool. They-- they cut off somebody's head, a kapo or something, you know, who-- who was a bastard. You know, they ganged up on him. And those things happen, you know, when some-- Who-- who did this, the German or the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, the people, this-- they-- they-- not-- There was no Jews. This wasn't a Jewish camp. Gross-Rosen was mostly-- I didn't see any Jews there, you know, especially the place where we were, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So who do you think was in Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe there were Jews, but there were different quarters. There was-- you know, this was a big camp. I saw this stone, the mountain of stone, and people walking around like they were-- they were that-- it was so far away, you know, they were-- they were one-foot tall. And walking those things, and-- and I never saw such a beautiful-- it-- it was a pink granite mountain. And they had to chop that granite or dynamite everything. And every time somebody doesn't work, he kicks them, and drops over.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it, but they were talking, you know, over there. And it's awful. I saw this all come out. If I ever have to work here, I'll never make it. And so they give us the spinach. There was-- I never liked home spinach, but, over there, you eat anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was half sand, half spinach, you know? And then grinding in my teeth and grinding. And then you cannot get it out, you know? Comes, later on, supper, what is there again, spinach. That was a week of spinach. So everybody ate spinach for, I don't know, probably for a week. And in the morning, spinach. The next day, spinach. And next, again, spinach. We didn't work. We were waiting for something. They didn't assign us to anything. So we figured, I think, we were just in a transit, whatever happens. And we there in the transit, in meantime, you know, they have to make exercise with us. Well, so it was our policemen-- they were there, too, you know-- where they came and-- you know, they didn't soften up yet. You know, you're here, they got--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they made us do up and down, you know. And every time you didn't bend down your face, and they used to kick your head in the mud, you know? And there is a few here where I told off, you know? They said, look, they told me to do it. I said, no, they didn't tell you to do it. You did it, you know? Are you talking about kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kapos? They wouldn't-- over there, there was nothing. They-- it was-- there was no set up yet. You understand? That was a transit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who were the ones who were giving--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the same. They were with us. Yeah, but they still were volunteers. They still had the-- their issues in them. You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: The cruelty.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the [NON-ENGLISH], you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sadism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So we were to do that, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. And-- and-- and that guy, you know, [NON-ENGLISH], you know? The one who did it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's in terrible trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's terrible trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now. Oh, the whole-- from even before, he had a beautiful wife. And they took girls, like 30 beautiful girls, I knew what-- probably for-- for their use or whatever it is. And then, like three months later, the girls came back in that one slate, to the-- to the place where they were shot in, over there, in Plazow. And one of his wives was there. His wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: His wives?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he got married here, poor guy. We happened to be at the wedding, too. And he had a mongoloid baby, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Down's syndrome?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Down's syndrome. Very smart, you know, in school with everything, but something awful to look at.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you think? Why do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what are you saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then the kid dies. Then his wife died. Then I saw him. And the last time I saw him, in Miami, in-- sitting by a bar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, Abe, you know, I'm-- I got A 35-foot boat. I say go ahead and jump with it, you know? I said, I don't want to go with you. You're not my friend and everything. And he's a lost soul. Matter of fact, he was on the list to come here. And I was looking for him. So you-- do you believe in God, Mr. Jachzel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, yes, in a way. No, I mean, there is something there, you know? You know, when my father comes in, in Plazow at that time, he say, you know what happened, you know, to Lewertow?

speaker: interviewer
text: They took him out. And they were shooting him, two, three of them. And every gun got stuck. You know, at the end, they kick him, and they-- and they walk away. I said, hey, [NON-ENGLISH], you know? Don't tell me stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's him. But Lewertow were working together in the same place and everything. Lewertow, Rav Lewertow, with a beard like this, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well he was his partner here in-- in United States, you know? And Lewertow was giving the wedding. My father did the singing. He made the brachots, you know, when they made weddings, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- my father always find the [NON-ENGLISH]. Well, that was $25--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father sang--

speaker: interviewee
text: --at that time It was almost as much as the minimum wage, whatever. You know? He had a couple a week. It was an extra $50, you know that?

speaker: interviewer
text: But the best one was, you know, after he had a little stroke and everything, that-- when he was making money, with the judges, with the-- with the-- with the lawyers, you know, teaching them, you know, learning Mishnayos. And you don't ask. You're not supposed to ask for money. They were just pushing it in his pocket. You know? So you-- you answered the question, half and half. Do you believe in God--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: --after the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: --but I'm not a fanatic about it, you know? But surely, I have to believe something. There-- there was some kind-- some kind of power. I don't know. I-- listen, I am not-- I-- I was never against it. Me, as a kid, it didn't go into my head. I was very hard to learn this thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was-- it-- it-- it bothered me, and I-- and I saw the-- the-- my cousins and everything, you know? Little Shloyme, you know? He knew already I have a [NON-ENGLISH], as you know, when he was a little kid, you know? As if the [NON-ENGLISH] And [NON-ENGLISH], he didn't mean me a German, he mean-- he wanted to be like I was, you know? We didn't want to-- we didn't want to have the pious-- you know? So when you asked this little brilliant cousin of yours, what do you want to be when you grow up? He says, I want to be German, like you, but not a German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a German. As a German, but, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: But not a payos--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a Hasidim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hasidishe kid.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, if you-- you-- you didn't wear the-- the-- the clothes and the [NON-ENGLISH], so you were a-- a-- a Deutsch, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But not the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Without belief.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] I-- I used to like that kid. You know, I always had an ice cream and this and that. And I don't know what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about when they-- when you went to Mr. Schindler's factory after Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went to Mr. Schindler's factory, then-- then was-- First of all, there were wires. There were old soldiers. And I figured, we can handle them, you know? And then I saw some of the SS came in from Gross-Rosen, who were the big things. But he keep them so drunk and occupied. And he said one thing, nobody comes into the shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Schindler said this?

speaker: interviewee
text: So you could see them-- Schindler, yes. Didn't let him them in. He said, there is machinery over there. And this bull with this, achtung-- every time every German comes in, somebody can chop his fingers off, can make a big accident. He said, no more. You don't come in. If you come in, as a-- a regularly, it's all right. Lunchtime, yes, after work, yes. He-- he had a good excuse, safety.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you taken out of Gross-Rosen to go to Mr Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was great. When in Gross-Rosen, when we were set up that we had to be going to Schindler, I think they give us something, a sandwich or something. You know, this was the first time in history of Gross-Rosen. It was like a-- a box lunch or whatever. But Schindler must have prepared for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: A box lunch?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little box. Yeah. This was a box lunch. It was a-- it was a sandwich with jam. And there was an apple. You know? It looked good, you know? So you know a feeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't close the-- the- the trains. You know, we were still in the same [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] train where I know my-- my-- from my mother's side, they were dealing with cattle, you know, from my mother's-- from-- You see, there were the poor people on my mother's side. There were very rich people, you know? So it was a fight. You know, it was always. So I-- I used to go with my grandfather and when they unloaded the-- the-- the cattle, you know, before the market. And he used to bring the two wagons. There were like 60, 30 in a-- And in the same cars, you know, we went-- You went.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from one camp to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not when you went to Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still, yes. But it was half open, you know, the door, you know? And I didn't see any wires out the window, you know, there's the-- It was-- it was a-- you-- you could see it. It-- it start getting a little humane. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was the trip from Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was an overnight trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Overnight. We came about 9 o'clock. And-- and then we saw the factory. And we saw two machines being unloaded. It had 200, 300-ton presses, you know? And to direct from the railroad-- the railroad was like a quarter mile away from the factory. And to direct a machine, a 300-ton press, and they didn't have the right idea, you know, how to. They were dug in-- in-- in the mud.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good that Ginter was there, my father. You know, they knew how to-- how to handle heavy-- heavy stuff, how to really put the rolls and where to put the blocks and, you know, to-- to schlep that thing, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you try to take the ropes and start schlepping and pushing, you know, it doesn't want to. It digs in deep into the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on the train that were going from Gross-Rosen?

speaker: interviewer
text: 700 Men?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 700 men, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of you had expertise in some toolmaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we did. Then we were like this. There were aces. And there were people who didn't know nothing. I remember, I-- I-- and, you know, one of the managers comes in. Listen, you're a smart kid. Can you teach Dr. Hilstein to-- to work a lathe?

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, sure, why not? Dr. Hilstein was a philanthropist. He was the main man for, you know, for hachsharas, for sending to Israel. Right, he was part of the Zionist--

speaker: interviewee
text: He must-- I don't know if he, himself, was a rich man. Or he was the one-- If you knew Dr. Hilstein, first of all, you could go to the camp when you were a little kid, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: To the [NON-ENGLISH] at your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a camp, just a Jewish camp from where they help out or there was money from United States. But they were beautiful. I went once to a camp in Rabka, a winter camp, you know? So even if you had money, you couldn't do that. Still, Dr. Hilstein has to get the OK, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he on the Kehilla?

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, my father-- What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in the Kehilla? Was he part of the Kehilla?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yeah, He was one of the-- he was a philanthropist. I knew that. And this, it was amazing. This Dr. Hilstein-- what was I, 20--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever-- whenever you're ready, you can continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Hilstein was an intelligent man, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: A very-- if anything, if any-- he knows. It's amazing how intelligent-- how people, who have knowledge, who-- who have-- I don't know he's a doctor or a dentist or whatever it is-- how fast they pick up to do something. You know? And it was a small lathe, a precision lathe. I give myself a few hours, myself, you know, to get acquainted with. And it was a job to do threadings, you know, screws, special screws. And I start doing them. And he was amazed with it. And he picked it up. About two, three days later, he did it. You know, I give him the next blueprint. He understood. He was so smart, that he could understand the print. It-- it-- fast, he could pick it up. That man, you know, to me, he was an old man. You know, I'm older than him now. You know, I feel old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he get to Schindler's factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, come on, this-- that's-- that's what it is. People, whatever it is, they still-- whoever was in the camp, or you was a kapo, or you was a policeman, he did have respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: We knew he cannot-- listen, those are people you have to respect. Without them, we nothing, if-- if we ever survive, you know? There was, with us, one, when there were a-- he was a minister of-- of law, of Poland, you know? When Goeth was there. So one of our men, who was with the-- on the Schindler's list, you know, was the judge, the main judge in town. You know? And he got on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: The minister of justice, OK? Yeah, he was with--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you making in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory had to be set up, complete. Forget about pots and pans, what they were doing in a-- in-- in Enmalia, over there, in-- in Plazow. This is out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this town--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to do-- we had to do the outer shell from a anti-aircraft bullet. So here comes a-- a new story, a new trade. I knew completely nothing, with people like me, my father, Gunter. Ginter knew how to set up. He was a master electrician. He set up the machines. You have to hook them up. You had to give power to them. My father knew how to bury the machines to bring them up, you know how much foundation they're going to need. You know, a 300-ton press needs like 8-foot square, all around, you know, certain stone and cement and so much pig iron and all whatever it is to set up the presses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they start setting up all the presses. And you know, and-- and what do you do with 700 people? You know, I think, all right, four lathes. One of them I work is number three, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was set up. But there must of be some kind of repair shop in that factory. the factory was like almost 200,000 square feet, 1 floor, 18-foot ceilings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One of the best heating systems I ever saw. There was like 10-inch pipes all around, you know, steam pipes. And it was nice and comfortable. You could wash something and just hang it up on the pipe.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was good. The only thing is, it was in a place, in-- in Czechoslovakia, where you could eat a horse. The air was so terrific over there. This is like-- [SIGHS] how can I tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you-- you-- you sending a sick man over there, he's going to get healthy, given enough food. It was-- The appetite was so great. And this is the first time. I was a very bad eater all my life, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Plazow didn't bother me. There were-- I had-- not that I had enough. But I had enough with the money, with everything we had. We ate. But over there, for some kind of reason, I never had cereal in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And over there, they-- you got a-- a cup of cereal in the morning. You know, my mother didn't make cereal in the morning. Take a piece of bread and butter. You never had time. You had to kick the ball, you know? And you're on. What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bruennlitz, yeah, this was the name of the town. And I understand the work, what we had right away, he must have get it from Skoda. It was only a mile or two away from us. And Skoda is the biggest factory in the world. Skoda, this was under one roof, they did everything, from-- from a car to a sewing machine, to-- to a bullet, to anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep? Where did all these 700 men sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the sleeping quarters, it was-- it was like this. Let's say there's 200,000 square feet was the place. You had a downstairs. And it was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] elongated. On one side was his quarters, Schindler, his wife. That was one side. We were upstairs. There were steps, you know, quite wide, something like 12-foot wide, you know, steps to go upstairs, concrete, everything solid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And upstairs, there were made up, like in Plazow, we had three layers. Over there, it was only two layers. You know? But the carpenters, who came with us, you know, or he prepared some. We finished it up, you know, to-- to make this. You know, they did the rest over the day, you know? And there was a lot of straw. He-- he-- he bought some straw there, you know? And some covers, whatever he had. And we slept. We didn't care. You know, this didn't mean nothing. We were-- after you sleep sardine-style, in Gross-Rosen, any bed is comfortable. Even now, I go and you know, I complain in Hilton, you know, I don't like the bed. You know? Did you sleep near to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you always stayed together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me. Yes. This already, yes. Here, we stay together. And he always with his mouth is, you know, advice. And got that hell from the [NON-ENGLISH]. So, you know, always got a full head. But, you know, never let-- you know, he knew that I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to translate, because what you said was really wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. You know, always [NON-ENGLISH] You know? [NON-ENGLISH] You know, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: His father-- his father kept telling him stories that said, soon the Messiah will come. And soon--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the-- and the war is going to end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the war is going to end. And your mother came to me in a dream. And she gave us-- was it cakes?

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought him kaesekuchen, yeah, cheesecake, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cheesecake, your mother brought. And don't worry, it's going to be over.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mr. Jachzel said, they're called mayses, which are stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were great philosophers that say that, if you believe this, you're crazy. But if you don't believe it, then you're a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you're a-- then you're a fool.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you are right. I used to say, you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. But we knew it already that it's the end. We didn't know how it's going to end. Are they going to come up, from over there, line us up and shoot us like dogs? You know, this we didn't know exactly how it's going to. We know it's going to end. We knew it, you know. There was a radio. Not-- we didn't have it. But the, you know, the guys, the kapos, whatever it is, and they were walking around like mice already.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew they were going to-- they're going to get hit. And some of them were, right away, arrested. You know? They were-- were brought back Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember. I was back in Cracow. And I see Dr. Gross, who-- well, you can blame now. He got 20 years, you know, seeing him with a-- schlepping from one jail to the other-- food for the-- from one criminal to the other, through the main streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this, do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was already in '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, yeah. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, Schindler, when you were in the factory of Schindler, was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: At Schindler, we went '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then May-- in May 8th, at-- the day the war ended, this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your liberator?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians came.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first you know, it-- it-- it's amazing. There comes a little kid, you know, maybe-- maybe-- he looked like 12, maybe he was 15, on a horse, naked. Naked horse, nothing on him, just the-- the-- the mouthpiece, you know, to hold him. You know? He comes in. He stands up. And he gives-- he-- he has a whole speech. You know, he was one they send out before anybody comes in. And he gives on a whole speech.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Russian, yeah. But you know, most of the guys, you know, when you know Polish, Russian, ain't so-- it's-- it's a-- it's easy to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't understand every word. Believe me, the words you have to understand, you know it already. And he holds a speech in-- Here, he come from one end. And we still see the Germans, the big tanks, the big trucks, loaded. And they're going, you know? The-- the town is shaking. They're going. They're going. They don't where to go. They go left or go right? Anyway, they go towards the-- towards the West, whatever it is, you know, away from the Russians. Then the Russians come in. It's unbelievable. I never saw this in my life. The whole carriage is this big. It's only got two lousy wheels, you know? And a horse, who looks half dead, you know? And they come in, with their beards. It-- it doesn't have-- even had a piece of leather to hold the-- the rifle. The rifle with a-- with a a piece of rope, you know? And dilapidated, dirty, and they started coming from the mountain, like this, in the thousands, like-- like-- like, you know, it's unbelievable. They-- they start coming in, not to us, but to take the town. I said, what is it? Here, they're running away, with-- with their heavy guns. The Germans are going?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole German damn thing, they run away. And here, look who's coming? Who they worrying about? All right. Anyway. Then comes in, about 11 o'clock in the afternoon, then comes the-- they came in, with a-- the trucks, the Russian trucks came in. So-- but they came light. They came with a radio, with the operators. And all of a sudden, there was a Russian-Jewish girl there. She was operating the-- the telephones and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she talks to the main quarters about us, and they ask us if we need food. And we we said, we had plenty food. By that time, Schindler said, you know, when I am leaving, this cow doesn't give anymore milk, you know? Mr. Schindler came down and told--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Mr. Schindler, there was the boys already took him out from the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What boys took him out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our fellows, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish boys, you know? There was Orbach was there. Another two, I think one of the Rosner kids, the one, he is very sick in cancer. I saw him last week, you know? I think he was with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He took Mr. Schindler and his wife out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There was Schindler, his wife, his girlfriend, you know the one? You know, she saved my-- my life once. And Goeth, he was hidden. He was with us in-- in-- in-- in the quarters. But Schindler told him, hey, [NON-ENGLISH], like you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lay down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't you ever show your face. I saw him only twice. So he like he live from 300 pounds, there was like left like 150. You know? But he was arrested by the Germans, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Amon, is that his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, Amon. Yeah, Amon, Amon, Amon, Amon Goeth. He must have be arrested. But they must have let him out when the Russians start coming. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying that Schindler, Mr. Schindler, his wife, his girlfriend, were taken out before the Russians came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- yeah, a day before.

speaker: interviewer
text: A day before.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a day before. Yeah. And I had a hard time, too, you know? And he left. We made him-- a week before he made that Schindler's list or two weeks before, we all signed it, how good he was to us. A thank you, whatever happens, happens. And this, somebody was hiding this-- that list. And then there was exact dates and everything. So we knew something is coming. You know, in case, they stop him, whatever it is, that he was one of the people who really want to help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe that he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'm sorry.
